speaker: interviewer
text: Today is September 16th, 1996. I'm interviewing survivor Fania Ingber Paszt. That's Fania, F-A-N-I-A, Ingber, I-N-G-B-E-R, Paszt, which is her maiden name, P-A-S-Z-T. My name is Rachel Alkallay, A-L-K-A-L-L-A-Y. We're conducting the interview in Montreal, Quebec, Canada. And we're doing the interview in English. Could I have your name, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Fania Ingber Paszt.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell it, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: F-A-N-I-A-- Fania Ingber-- I-N-G-B-E-R, and Paszt-- P-A-S-Z-T.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Fania.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, but your maiden name. Your last name was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Paszt.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever have any other nicknames?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or any other name that you were known by?

speaker: interviewee
text: Known by-- [NON-ENGLISH] in Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could I have your birth date, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: The 1st of April, 1922.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you are hold old now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now I am 40-- sorry. Now I'm going to be 74.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is the city of your birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Luck-- L-U-T-S-K.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Poland-- Poland, Used to be.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, in the town of Luck, where your family came from--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your family came from Luck. What was it like? What was the town like? Was it a big place?

speaker: interviewee
text: How big? It was a big town. This was the capital of Wolyn.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was more of a city--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --than a town. What was the Jewish population?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't-- I can't tell you exactly. I know this-- 20,000 Jews were killed during the war. And how many exactly, I can't tell you. I don't know. It was more.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were the Jews the majority in the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: I beg your pardon.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were the Jews a majority in the town of Luck? Were they-- were there more Jews in Luck than Christians, or more Christians than Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I can't tell you. I don't know. I think it was a Christian. It was Jewish. It was enough. It was a nice Jewish population.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the Jews live in one place, or were they all over?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, all over, all over they city.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of what did the Jews do there? What were their professions?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jews, you know, is stores and they work, tailors and shoemakers, all kinds, like always. They find their things to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they generally well-to-do? Did they have money?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or were there poor Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It's everything [INAUDIBLE] some of them poor, some of them rich, some are them medium class.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the community well-organized?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, it was a nice community-- a very nice community.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did people go to-- how was the religious life of the Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, they were really very, you know, observant Orthodox, all kind. But they were OK. They were organized. They were mainly orthodox? Did you have any reformed Jews? There was-- I don't know. Probably it was. I don't know, because my father was an Orthodox. That's why I know and I remember, but what about the others I can't tell you.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you-- do you recall Shabbat at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was Shabbat like in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: At home-- a real Shabbat. Friday night, Saturday, Daddy used to go to shul, take the son, the older one. The younger one, I don't remember. But it was a Shabbat-- prepared everything like it has to be.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did the table look like-- the Shabbat table that your mother prepared?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Shabbat table, it looked beautiful. It was holly. It was candles on the table. it was everything how it's supposed to be for Shabbat. We had a nice home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother-- she made the blessings over the candles always?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah, always, every Friday in Shabbat nobody use to do anything and relaxed.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your mother look like?

speaker: interviewee
text: She looked-- she looked beautiful. But I don't have a picture, sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you-- can you describe her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh she was a nice lady-- tall, nice, slim.

speaker: interviewer
text: What color hair did she have?

speaker: interviewee
text: Dark one-- brownish.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her eyes?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's also, I think, brownish. I [INAUDIBLE]. I know that father had blue eyes because he-- my children has it. They-- they look like my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did-- did your mother wear a sheitel?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. She wore the hair. She had long hair. And she used to all around, you know, to take them and to make, you know, like, with a bow or sometimes with pins in the back. This I do remember-- no sheitel.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did she-- was she very-- was she fashionable, your mother, or did she dress Orthodox?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was Orthodox, but not too, but she was.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have any brothers or sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I had two brothers.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, the oldest was Pinchas. And the youngest was Berl.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you get along with your brothers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I forgot already, a long time, but we did.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were-- with your brothers, what kind of child would you say you were? Were you a quiet child? Were you a tomboy?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, we were quiet, all of us. We were quiet.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do to-- to pass the time, when you weren't studying? What did you do-- what hobbies?

speaker: interviewee
text: What hobby? I don't remember any hobbies, but I was busy always. If not the boys, then the girls, and they used to go to school, come back and go to organization.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of organization?

speaker: interviewee
text: [HEBREW]

speaker: interviewer
text: You were--

speaker: interviewee
text: And my brother belonged there also-- the bigger, older one.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- you became [HEBREW] why, because it was an organization in town, or you wanted to make aliyah, or?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not [INAUDIBLE] I'm told. I was too young. But my brother went there. And all the friends used to come to the house, and I went with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: What language did they conduct the meetings in for [HEBREW]?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yiddish and Hebrew.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you knew Hebrew?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You studied that in school? What was your education like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah I studied in school. But I forgot. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of school did you go to?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to [HEBREW].

speaker: interviewer
text: And so that's where you got your basic Jewish education.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you go to any other school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I went to-- when got older, went to public into high school.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was public--

speaker: interviewee
text: But a few-- a few grades.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was public school like? It was in Polish?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's like Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have to learn religious classes? Did you have to take--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, it was religious classes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you have to go to them, being--

speaker: interviewee
text: The same. Were the same-- in the same time used to have, let's say, an hour a week. I don't remember how it worked.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it Jewish studies or Catholic studies?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Jewish. How-- did you have any friends who weren't Jewish, any Christian friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, most I think they were Jewish, because it was like a district, you know, with most of them. I didn't-- no, I think most of them were Jewish from the school.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how were-- how was the relations between Jews and Christians in-- in Luck?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Luck it was always, like everywhere. It was the same. Some of them were good. Some of them were bad, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: When they were bad, how were they bad?

speaker: interviewee
text: It [INAUDIBLE] anti-Semitism, but I don't-- I didn't have anything to do with this.

speaker: interviewer
text: You never experienced it personally?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, never. We had some neighbors, and everybody was OK. So I can't tell exactly how it was, and I don't remember it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did Did you ever visit your neighbor's house, or did your neighbors ever visit your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah, there is-- it was altogether, you know. We used to live one house. It's not like here, you live in a condo or an apartment. It was everybody had their own home-- most. And we had a house. And it was two more-- one two-- two more tenants, but they were Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your home? What was your home like? Could you describe it, what your house looked like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like a one-family house with a basement downstairs, not a living basement, but a basement to keep for the winter and for the summer and dairy and all kind of things, because we didn't have a fridge.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was there were a lot of furniture in your apartment, or in your house, in your home? Were there many furnishings? Was it--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it was. It was a dining room. It was bedrooms. [INAUDIBLE] I think two or three bedrooms and a dining room. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Yeah two-- two bedrooms and a dining room, a big one, a kitchen when you walked in into our-- before you know, like a, hallway, a small one. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are your favorite memories of that time, of that time in your life before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any favorite memories?

speaker: interviewee
text: From war, oh no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: From before the war-- what kind of memories? I lost everybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you don't--

speaker: interviewee
text: I have them in mine. That's all. And the rest I don't care about-- the furniture and all this. It was everything. It was nice and beautiful and clean. But I don't-- the house-- I didn't even going into the house after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was-- with your father, he was religious, also.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he follow a particular rabbi?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. He had a shul, which it was the rabbi-- the name of the rabbi who used to come there or [NON-ENGLISH] rabbi. I don't know. I never saw him. But I know the shul was named [HEBREW]. Scrappy

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he an important rabbi?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know anything about him, about the rabbi, or?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I used to go away for the holidays in shul with Mama, but I don't know anything about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the shul like? Where did the women sit?

speaker: interviewee
text: A nice shul. A nice shul. The women used to sit upstairs. You have to walk in, and stairs, and the men downstairs. It was a nice shul-- a very, very nice shul.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it very fancy inside, or very plain?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was nice. I don't remember how fancy it was. I didn't know what can be, you know, the difference. But it was a nice shul-- a nice and a big shul.

speaker: interviewer
text: A big shul-- do you know how many people could sit there, or?

speaker: interviewee
text: I never paid attention.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you like going to synagogue, to shul? Did you like going?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I went with the mother, sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: You liked it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My mother didn't go every Saturday, but she used to go from time to time. But for the holidays, she used to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you always kept kosher.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. There was not a thing not kosher.

speaker: interviewer
text: When the war began, in 1939, how did-- where were you at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, by us it was the Germans. The Russians came in '39. And then they left. The Germans came. In--

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did the Russians stay?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Russians were maybe a year, year and a half, or two years, something like this. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was life like under the Russians?

speaker: interviewee
text: With the Russians everybody used to go to work, and they tried to close, you know, some stores, and the people used to work for-- you know, for the Russian, like-- like in Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they tried to make it like a communist kind of society?

speaker: interviewee
text: They tried. Maybe they did succeed by some. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: When the Russians were in charge, did you feel any anti-Semitism? Was there any anti-Semitism? I don't think so. I didn't feel like-- I don't know. I don't want to say anything because I don't know. And I don't remember the [? true ?] things. When the Russians left, and the Germans came in--

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans came in, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Germans kicked out the Russians, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a war, so they left.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then the Germans came in? This was around when? [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewee
text: 1941, I think. Yes, in '41. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] In '41, I think. And then the Germans came, and they took right away my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they take him--

speaker: interviewee
text: After a couple of days.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they take him away, were you at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: They take him away because-- yeah, everybody was home. But he was standing in the window. And some Ukrainian came over-- Ukrainian police-- came over and through the window they made to him like this. So he went over to the bed, and he took the [INAUDIBLE] with the Star of David and the jacket, and they took him away. And that's it. It was the 13 of August. It was 3:30 in the afternoon in 1941.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know where they took him to?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know probably to the Gestapo, or they killed him. Who knows? It's not only him they took. They took a few people there that died. They used to take always. But he was from the first one. And we never saw him again.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said that the Ukrainian police had taken him away.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, Ukrainian police came.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Ukrainians were working with the Germans, or?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, the Ukrainians were working for the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a lot of Ukrainian anti-semitism that you felt from the soldiers-- [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't feel anything, but when they came in, so then we don't-- if we didn't see them, then we went to the ghetto. Sure, the Ukrainian worked together with the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were they-- who would you say behaved in a worse way, the Germans or the Ukrainians?

speaker: interviewee
text: Both. Who did it, I don't know. But they are both-- it was the Ukrainian helped the Germans, and they worked together. There is no difference who. The most important, they took away, and that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: When-- were there are other nationalities working with the Germans besides the Ukrainians?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Polish. The Polish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Also who was able worked for them, sure. Everybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your neighbors, did they work with the Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who?

speaker: interviewer
text: Your neighbors-- your neighbors in the town-- did they help the Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they-- no older people, no, they didn't, no. But some people, they worked. Some who had a chance worked with the-- who paid. They'd work for them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the Germans would pay the Poles to work with them.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah, for sure they pay. For nothing, they didn't work, and maybe.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened? You mentioned ghetto. When did the ghetto in Luck begin? The ghetto began before the winter. It was maybe in September or something. I don't remember when. But then they took everybody into one place-- all the Jews had to go. That was in what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hm?

speaker: interviewer
text: In what year?

speaker: interviewer
text: So they--

speaker: interviewer
text: They brought all the Jews to one place.

speaker: interviewee
text: To one place, yes, on one side of the city.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a place where Jews usually lived?

speaker: interviewee
text: They used to live, yeah, usually, yeah. There was the ghetto, and everybody went.

speaker: interviewer
text: How big was the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hm?

speaker: interviewer
text: How many-- how big? How many streets? How many homes? I don't-- I never was there before the war. No but during the war, how big was the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, the [NON-ENGLISH] ghetto was quite big. You know-- one part of the city.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how was it separated from the rest of the city, the ghetto? Was there a fence around it, or?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. No, it was not fence. But you couldn't go there. It police, you know. It was a special place where-- how you called it? There was something like a bridge, a small one. So nobody went, but everybody was afraid.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there were guards guarding this bridge?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, and then it was there-- in the ghetto was the hospital. It was a hospital there, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] which it was the hospital was there before the war also.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it equipped, the hospital, like a normal hospital? Did it have--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, not a-- not a big one, because we had-- we had hospitals, a few of them. But this one was a small one. And-- [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were the doctors and nurses there? Were there-- were they Jewish? Were they Poles?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a few-- a few Jewish doctors that was in the town, and it was nurses Jewish and Polish, oh yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so there were Polish doctors and nurses working in the Jewish hospital in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the ghetto, yes. It was there. It was another hospital, a big one. But this was a small one. It was special, I think, for the-- for the ghetto people-- a small one.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in the ghetto, who did they put in? Were they only the Jews from Luck, or were there other Jews from other places?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Only from Luck. But when they killed, they-- I think they got some from the small villages also to into Luck, and then-- then they killed already. When it was the [INAUDIBLE], you know, to kill the Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what was life like in the ghetto? What did you do in the ghetto? In the ghetto, nothing. Just yak, yak, and they said like this, and they said like that. The German going to go. The Russian going to come back and the opposite, you know. But nothing special. But there was no work to be done?

speaker: interviewee
text: For some people, they worked, who, you know-- if they need some people to go to work, they used to give the Judenrat that they have-- they need let's say for today 10 people, 20 people to this place, to the other place, you know. And they used to go. They used to get some, separately, some passes to get out from the ghetto and [INAUDIBLE] the identification.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you mentioned the Judenrat--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was in charge of the Judenrat?

speaker: interviewee
text: It used to-- Jewish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: But do you know who was head of it, or do you know any of the names of the people on the Judenrat?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, I don't know. I don't remember. I knew them, but I don't remember the names. But they're Jewish people in the Jewish police force.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who chose the Jewish police?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Judenrat. They-- or they went, you know, by themself. They wanted to be in the police, because they thought when they're going to kill, so the police they won't touch. But it was the opposite.

speaker: interviewer
text: But they did different things to the-- to the police also.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Oh, yeah, sure. They went together.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did the Jewish population in the ghetto feel about the Judenrat? They had to follow them. If the Judenrat gave an order, you have to fill out the order. What can they do? The Judenrat used to give the ration. How much chea bread to give for every family, they used to give the Judenrat, and how much sugar, all this. Everything went through there. They had to feed a little bit. When you were in the ghetto, were you able to celebrate Shabbat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who was thinking about this? Yeah, sometimes, you know. Who wants to light, who was able to light a candle? See, some people, they had the candles and some not. They used to take, like, a piece of string to make like a candle. But there was no law, because [INAUDIBLE] nobody can afford this. The people who remained in their houses, so sometimes you had there a few candles, spare one, and sometimes not. It's not like here. You prepare. You buy a big box, so you have it for a whole year. In Poland it was not like this. If you need two candles, you went out and buy two candles.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother-- when you were in the ghetto, did your mother light?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. She used to. She used to light and cried. She used to cry. That's all. Who knows? Who knows? Who knows where is Daddy? Who knows where is my husband? That's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your brothers? Where were they at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were home.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were with you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they able to do any kind of work also, where they ever called?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, My older brother, he was married. He was with his wife. And the younger was with us. Then they took him to away, and we also-- we didn't know where he went. Some people-- he ran away. Some people said that they saw him, and he had frozen fingers and feet. But he was not alive. And the older got killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was he killed? Do you know? Do--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they shot him. He was running. And he was wearing, like, officer boots, you know, stiff one, nice ones, so. And he couldn't jump or something. Anyway, they killed him. I know. This I know for sure. They find him dead.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said that he was wearing officer boots. Was he in the army?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, but at the shul, he [INAUDIBLE]. So he had nice shoes. We all had nice shoes. There it was the style.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had nice shoes. What did you look like at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who, me?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Like a Gentile, because I had two long pigtails.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your coloring like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hm?

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your coloring like?

speaker: interviewee
text: The coloring? The face, I think the same. You know, but younger, and that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you looked like a Gentile?

speaker: interviewee
text: A Gentile.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it easier for you to get by that you looked like a Gentile, or it didn't make a difference?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I run. Maybe that's why I am alive. I don't know. Who made it-- not me. I didn't choose to escape or to-- if somebody would ask me, they'd say, oh, you're going to survive. You will survive. I would never say yes. It came accidentally. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people were living in your-- in your apartment in the ghetto? Was it just you and your mother and your-- and your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh no, there was more. A few families was there. It was a big house-- not in our house. It's in somebody's. It was a big house, and there's was a couple of families.

speaker: interviewer
text: And everybody-- every family lived in their own room?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Wherever it was a place to sleep. There was no room. It was no [INAUDIBLE]. It was not a hotel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did people share--

speaker: interviewee
text: Believe me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did people share food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, no. I don't think so. You have a potato. You ate. It was very hard. When you were alone and very hungry, it was hard to share. When you have to look at the piece of bread to leave it, to eat it now, or to have it later.

speaker: interviewer
text: Maybe we'll take a break now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hm?

speaker: interviewer
text: Maybe we'll take a break-- a little break.

speaker: interviewee
text: You want to take a break?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't mind.
